pattern: A sum-over-subsets max table lets you query, for any value, the largest array element whose set bits avoid it, turning the disjoint-bit pair search into a linear bitmask scan.
hints: Two numbers share no set bits exactly when one's bit pattern is a subset of the other's complement.
If you could instantly ask "what is the biggest array value whose bits all lie inside this mask?", each element would just query the complement of its own bits.
Build f[mask] = largest array value whose set bits are a subset of mask using a sum-over-subsets DP, then for every element multiply it by f[FULL XOR element] and keep the best product (0 if none).

edge_inputs.description: mixed small values, best pair is 3 and 4
every pair overlaps in bits, answer is 0
all powers of two are mutually disjoint
minimum length, two disjoint powers of two
minimum length but the only pair overlaps
large disjoint values near the cap
duplicate large value cannot pair with itself (shares bits)

stress.description: many values over a moderate range so disjoint pairs and overlaps both appear
stress.note: moderate bit width keeps the SOS table small while exercising many disjoint pairs
